articles.headline: Supreme Court Reaffirms Transparency in Electoral Funding, Sets New Guidelines
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark ruling, emphasized the paramount importance of transparency in electoral funding to uphold democratic principles.
• The judgment directed the Election Commission of India (ECI) to formulate new, stringent guidelines for disclosure of political donations.
• This ruling comes as a follow-up to previous judgments and aims to close loopholes in existing electoral finance laws.
• The court highlighted the 'right to know' of citizens regarding sources of political funding as an integral part of free and fair elections.
• Implications include potential reforms in the Representation of the People Act and increased accountability for political parties.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
Salient features of the Representation of People's Act.
Role of Election Commission of India.
prelimsPoints: The Supreme Court's ruling impacts the Election Commission of India's powers and responsibilities.
The judgment refers to previous rulings on electoral transparency, potentially including the 2018 and 2024 judgments on electoral bonds.
Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution (Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression) is often cited in 'right to know' cases.
The Representation of the People Act, 1951 governs elections in India.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the impact of the Supreme Court's verdict on the financial transparency of political parties and its role in strengthening Indian democracy.
Discuss the challenges in implementing comprehensive electoral finance reforms in India, considering the interplay of political will, legal frameworks, and practical enforcement.
Examine how the 'right to know' regarding political funding aligns with and potentially strengthens other fundamental rights guaranteed by the Indian Constitution.
Evaluate the role of the Election Commission of India in ensuring free and fair elections, particularly in light of judicial pronouncements on electoral transparency.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of the Supreme Court of India building with scales of justice, symbolizing judicial pronouncements on electoral transparency.
articles.trivia: The first general elections in independent India were held from October 1951 to February 1952, under the provisions of the Representation of the People Act, 1951.

articles.headline: Major Anti-Naxal Operation in Chhattisgarh Claims Lives of 30 Naxalites and One Security Personnel
articles.explainer:
• Intense clashes erupted between security forces and Naxalites in Dantewada and Kanker districts of Chhattisgarh.
• The operation resulted in the neutralization of 30 Naxalites, a significant blow to the insurgent group's presence in the region.
• Tragically, one military personnel also lost their life during the fierce encounter.
• The operation involved joint forces of state police and central paramilitary units, acting on specific intelligence inputs.
• Authorities have intensified combing operations in the affected areas, underscoring ongoing efforts to curb left-wing extremism.
gsSubTopics: Linkages between development and spread of extremism.
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security.

Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
prelimsPoints: Dantewada and Kanker districts are located in the Bastar region of Chhattisgarh, known for Naxalite activities.
Left-Wing Extremism (LWE) is primarily concentrated in the 'Red Corridor' regions of India.
Security forces involved in anti-Naxal operations include CRPF, BSF, ITBP, and state police forces.
Operation Green Hunt was a major anti-Naxal offensive launched by the government in 2009-10.
mainsPoints: Analyze the socio-economic factors contributing to the persistence of Left-Wing Extremism (LWE) in regions like Chhattisgarh, and suggest comprehensive strategies for addressing them.
Discuss the effectiveness of current security strategies in combating Naxalism. What role do development initiatives play alongside kinetic operations?
Examine the challenges faced by security forces in anti-Naxal operations, including terrain, local support, and logistical issues.
Critically evaluate the 'Surrender and Rehabilitation Policy' for Naxalites. How effective has it been in de-radicalizing and reintegrating former cadres?
articles.imageDescription: Map highlighting Dantewada and Kanker districts in Chhattisgarh, symbolizing the ongoing security challenges in the region.
articles.trivia: The term 'Naxalite' originated from the village of Naxalbari in West Bengal, where a peasant uprising took place in 1967.

articles.headline: India-Japan 2+2 Dialogue Concludes, Strengthening Indo-Pacific Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-Japan 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue involving Foreign and Defence Ministers concluded in Tokyo.
• Discussions focused on enhancing bilateral security and defence cooperation, including joint exercises and technology sharing.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region, countering unilateral attempts to change the status quo.
• Key agreements were reached on expanding cooperation in emerging technologies, cyber security, and critical infrastructure.
• The dialogue also addressed regional and global issues, including climate change and counter-terrorism efforts.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: The 2+2 Dialogue involves the Foreign and Defence Ministers of both countries.
India has 2+2 dialogues with the USA, Japan, Australia, Russia, and the UK.
Japan is a key partner in the Quad grouping, alongside India, the USA, and Australia.
The 'Act East Policy' is a cornerstone of India's engagement with Southeast Asian and East Asian countries, including Japan.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the India-Japan strategic partnership in ensuring stability and prosperity in the Indo-Pacific region. How does this partnership contribute to India's 'Act East Policy'?
Discuss the evolving dimensions of defence and security cooperation between India and Japan. What are the potential areas for further collaboration, especially in emerging technologies?
Evaluate the role of 2+2 dialogues as a diplomatic mechanism for enhancing bilateral relations and addressing complex regional and global challenges.
Examine how India's bilateral relations with key partners like Japan align with its broader geopolitical objectives and its stance on multilateral forums.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan side-by-side, symbolizing strong bilateral ties and cooperation in the Indo-Pacific.
articles.trivia: Japan is a significant investor in India's infrastructure projects, including the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail (Bullet Train) project.

articles.headline: Prime Minister Launches Phase II of PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana, Targets 2 Crore Households
articles.explainer:
• The Prime Minister today launched the second phase of the PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana, a flagship rooftop solar scheme.
• This phase aims to cover an additional 2 crore households, providing free electricity and promoting renewable energy adoption.
• The scheme offers significant subsidies and collateral-free bank loans for installing rooftop solar systems.
• It is expected to boost employment generation in the renewable energy sector and reduce India's carbon footprint.
• Emphasis was placed on achieving energy self-sufficiency and reducing electricity bills for economically weaker sections.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana is a central sector scheme.
The scheme provides financial assistance for installing rooftop solar panels.
It aims to provide up to 300 units of free electricity per month to eligible households.
The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) is the nodal ministry for this scheme.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the potential of rooftop solar programs like PM-Surya Ghar to address India's energy demands and contribute to its climate change commitments. What are the associated challenges?
Discuss the socio-economic benefits of promoting decentralized renewable energy generation, especially for rural and economically vulnerable populations.
Examine the role of government subsidies and financial incentives in accelerating the adoption of renewable energy technologies in India.
Analyze the impact of such schemes on India's energy security and its transition away from fossil fuels.
articles.imageDescription: An illustration of solar panels on rooftops of houses, symbolizing the PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana.
articles.trivia: India's installed renewable energy capacity (excluding large hydro) is the 4th largest in the world.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches New Ocean Monitoring Satellite for Climate Research
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched a new state-of-the-art ocean monitoring satellite aboard the PSLV-C59.
• The satellite, named 'OceanSat-4', is designed to provide critical data for oceanography, climate studies, and marine weather forecasting.
• It carries advanced sensors for measuring sea surface temperature, ocean color, and wind vectors, vital for understanding ocean dynamics.
• This mission reinforces India's capabilities in remote sensing and its commitment to global climate action.
• The data from OceanSat-4 will be crucial for disaster management, fisheries, and coastal zone management.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) is ISRO's third-generation launch vehicle.
OceanSat-4 is part of India's series of ocean observation satellites.
The satellite will contribute to international efforts for ocean monitoring and climate modeling.
ISRO operates from the Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC) in Sriharikota, Andhra Pradesh.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's space program, particularly its Earth Observation Satellites, in addressing critical national challenges such as climate change, disaster management, and resource mapping.
Evaluate ISRO's role in advancing scientific research and technological innovation. How does its indigenization efforts contribute to India's self-reliance in space technology?
Examine the applications of satellite data in oceanography and its importance for sustainable development, including fisheries management and coastal protection.
Analyze the global cooperation in space exploration and climate research, highlighting India's contributions and collaborations.
articles.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of the OceanSat-4 satellite orbiting Earth, with oceans visible below.
articles.trivia: ISRO was formed in 1969, succeeding the Indian National Committee for Space Research (INCOSPAR), which was established in 1962.

articles.headline: Draft National Water Policy 2025 Emphasizes Integrated River Basin Management
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Jal Shakti released the draft National Water Policy 2025 for public consultation.
• The policy prioritizes integrated river basin management, inter-state water cooperation, and demand-side management.
• It proposes a shift from a supply-side approach to water management to one focused on conservation, reuse, and efficiency.
• Climate change adaptation, groundwater recharge, and protection of water bodies are key pillars of the new policy.
• The draft also advocates for greater community participation and use of modern technologies for water governance.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: The Ministry of Jal Shakti was formed in 2019 by merging the Ministry of Water Resources, River Development & Ganga Rejuvenation and Ministry of Drinking Water and Sanitation.
India's first National Water Policy was adopted in 1987, revised in 2002 and 2012.
Integrated River Basin Management aims to coordinate management of water, land, and related resources across a river basin.
The concept of 'water neutrality' or 'water positive' is gaining traction.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the proposed National Water Policy 2025. How far does it address India's persistent water challenges, including scarcity, pollution, and inter-state disputes?
Discuss the importance of shifting from a supply-side to a demand-side approach in water management, emphasizing the role of community participation and technological innovation.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in implementing integrated river basin management, particularly in the context of India's federal structure and diverse hydrological regions.
Evaluate the potential impact of climate change on India's water resources and how the new policy aims to build resilience against these impacts.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized map of India with major rivers highlighted, symbolizing integrated water resource management.
articles.trivia: India accounts for about 18% of the world's population but has only 4% of the world's renewable water resources.

articles.headline: Gender Budgeting Report 2024-25 Highlights Progress and Gaps in Women-Centric Schemes
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Finance released its annual Gender Budgeting Statement as part of the Union Budget 2024-25.
• The report details allocations for schemes specifically targeting women's empowerment and gender equality across various ministries.
• While there's an increase in overall allocation, the report also identified critical gaps in implementation and outreach.
• Key focus areas included women's health, education, economic participation, and safety.
• The analysis emphasized the need for better data collection and outcome-based monitoring for gender-responsive budgeting.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
prelimsPoints: Gender Budgeting is a tool to ensure that government policies and programs address gender inequalities.
It is not a separate budget but an analysis of the government's main budget from a gender perspective.
The first Gender Budget Statement in India was introduced in 2005-06.
Part A of the Gender Budget Statement covers schemes 100% for women; Part B covers schemes where at least 30% of allocation is for women.
mainsPoints: Discuss the concept of Gender Budgeting and its evolution in India. How effective has it been in promoting gender equality and women's empowerment?
Analyze the key findings of the latest Gender Budgeting Report, highlighting both the successes and the persistent challenges in achieving gender-responsive development outcomes.
Examine the role of various stakeholders, including government ministries, civil society organizations, and local communities, in ensuring effective implementation of women-centric schemes.
Suggest reforms and policy measures to strengthen gender budgeting mechanisms and improve the impact of welfare schemes for women in India.
articles.imageDescription: Infographic showing various sectors like health, education, and economy, with a focus on women, representing gender budgeting.
articles.trivia: The idea of gender budgeting originated in Australia in the mid-1980s.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Inflation Concerns, Focus on Growth
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) decided to keep the repo rate unchanged at its current level.
• The decision was influenced by persistent inflation concerns, particularly in food and core inflation, despite some moderation.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to withdrawing accommodation to ensure inflation aligns with the target while supporting growth.
• Analysts anticipate continued vigilance from the RBI, with future rate actions dependent on inflation trajectory and economic data.
• The Governor highlighted global economic uncertainties and geopolitical tensions as key risks to domestic growth and inflation outlook.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body, with three members from the RBI and three external members appointed by the government.
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
The target for inflation is 4% with a band of +/- 2% (2% to 6%).
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks in the event of any shortfall of funds.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind the RBI's decision to maintain the status quo on the repo rate. How does this decision balance the objectives of inflation control and economic growth?
Discuss the challenges faced by the Monetary Policy Committee in managing inflation amidst global supply chain disruptions and domestic demand pressures.
Examine the various tools of monetary policy employed by the RBI and their effectiveness in achieving macro-economic stability.
Evaluate the impact of the RBI's monetary policy stance on various sectors of the Indian economy, including banking, industry, and consumption.
articles.imageDescription: The Reserve Bank of India building with a graph showing stable interest rates, representing monetary policy decisions.
articles.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

articles.headline: Review of National Education Policy 2020 Implementation Highlights Progress and Implementation Hurdles
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education released an interim review report on the implementation progress of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• The report noted significant strides in curriculum reforms, digital education initiatives, and skill development programs.
• However, it also pointed out challenges in teacher training, infrastructure development, and equitable access in remote areas.
• The review emphasized the need for greater state-level coordination and resource allocation to achieve NEP's ambitious goals.
• Recommendations included accelerating the establishment of multidisciplinary institutions and strengthening early childhood care and education.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: The National Education Policy 2020 replaced the 1986 policy.
NEP 2020 aims for universalization of education from pre-school to secondary level.
It proposes a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure instead of the 10+2 system.
The policy emphasizes vocational education and multidisciplinary learning.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the progress and challenges in the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020. What specific reforms are crucial for its successful realization?
Discuss how NEP 2020 aims to transform India's education system to meet 21st-century demands, focusing on aspects like skill development, digital literacy, and holistic learning.
Examine the role of technology and digital initiatives in enhancing educational access and quality as envisioned by the NEP. What are the associated equity concerns?
Analyze the importance of teacher training and capacity building for the effective implementation of the new pedagogical approaches proposed by NEP 2020.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing the transformative goals of the National Education Policy.
articles.trivia: The Kothari Commission (1964-66) was instrumental in shaping India's first National Policy on Education in 1968.

articles.headline: India-Pakistan Hold Talks on Indus Water Treaty, Addressing Technical Issues
articles.explainer:
• Delegations from India and Pakistan met for their annual Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) talks under the Indus Water Treaty (IWT).
• The discussions primarily focused on technical issues related to various hydroelectric projects on the western rivers.
• India reiterated its commitment to the treaty while asserting its right to utilize its share of water resources for non-consumptive uses.
• Pakistan raised concerns over the design of certain Indian projects, which India clarified were within treaty provisions.
• The talks are crucial for maintaining peace and cooperation over shared water resources, despite broader bilateral tensions.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: The Indus Water Treaty was signed in 1960, brokered by the World Bank.
It allocates the waters of the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and the Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan.
India has the right to use the waters of the Western Rivers for non-consumptive purposes like power generation.
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is a bilateral commission that implements and manages the treaty.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical significance and continued relevance of the Indus Water Treaty (IWT) in managing water resources between India and Pakistan. What makes it one of the most successful water treaties globally?

Analyze the challenges and complexities in implementing the IWT, particularly concerning the construction of hydroelectric projects on the Western Rivers. How does India balance its development needs with treaty obligations?
Examine the role of water diplomacy in mitigating tensions between riparian states. How can the IWT serve as a model for resolving other transboundary water disputes?
Evaluate the potential impact of climate change on the Indus Basin and the implications for the long-term sustainability of the IWT.
articles.imageDescription: Map of the Indus River system showing the allocation of rivers between India and Pakistan, symbolizing the Indus Water Treaty.
articles.trivia: The Indus Water Treaty is one of the very few international treaties that have survived multiple wars and conflicts between the signatory nations.

articles.headline: National Digital Health Mission Expands Reach, Focuses on Telemedicine Integration
articles.explainer:
• The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM) announced plans to expand its services to cover more remote and rural areas.
• A key focus of the expansion is the seamless integration of telemedicine services into the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) framework.
• This aims to improve access to healthcare, reduce geographical barriers, and enhance the efficiency of medical consultations.
• The mission emphasized creating a robust digital health ecosystem, including health IDs, health professional registries, and health facility registries.
• Training programs for healthcare workers on digital platforms are being scaled up to support the increased adoption of digital health solutions.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM) was launched in August 2020, later renamed Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM).
It aims to create a digital health ecosystem in India.
A key component is the Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) number, a unique health ID.
e-Sanjeevani is a national telemedicine service managed by the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of the National Digital Health Mission (ABDM) in revolutionizing healthcare delivery in India, particularly in terms of accessibility, efficiency, and equity.
Analyze the role of telemedicine in bridging the rural-urban divide in healthcare and its specific applications during public health crises.
Examine the challenges in implementing a comprehensive digital health ecosystem in India, including data privacy concerns, digital literacy, and infrastructure gaps.
Suggest measures to ensure the inclusivity and robustness of the National Digital Health Mission, making it beneficial for all sections of society.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting a doctor consulting a patient via a digital screen, symbolizing telemedicine and digital health.
articles.trivia: India has one of the largest populations of internet users, which provides a strong foundation for digital health initiatives.

articles.headline: Government Unveils New Policy for Exploration and Mining of Critical Minerals
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Mines announced a new policy framework aimed at accelerating the exploration and mining of critical minerals in India.
• The policy introduces incentives for private sector participation and foreign investment in deep-seated and strategic mineral blocks.
• It emphasizes sustainable mining practices, environmental protection, and local community engagement.
• The move is crucial for reducing India's import dependence on critical minerals like lithium, cobalt, and rare earth elements.
• The policy seeks to bolster domestic manufacturing capabilities in sectors like electric vehicles, renewable energy, and advanced electronics.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Critical minerals are essential for economic development and national security, especially in high-tech industries.
India heavily relies on imports for many critical minerals.
The Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Act, 1957, is the principal legislation governing mining in India.
The Geological Survey of India (GSI) is the primary body for geological mapping and mineral exploration.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of critical minerals for India's economic growth and national security. How does the new mining policy aim to achieve self-reliance in this sector?
Analyze the challenges involved in the exploration and extraction of critical minerals in India, including environmental concerns, technological gaps, and regulatory hurdles.
Evaluate the role of private sector participation and foreign investment in boosting India's critical mineral supply chain. What incentives are crucial for attracting such investments?
Examine the linkages between critical mineral availability and the growth of key industries like electric vehicles, renewable energy, and advanced electronics in India.
articles.imageDescription: Illustrative image of various critical minerals (lithium, cobalt, rare earths) arranged around a map of India, symbolizing their strategic importance.
articles.trivia: India recently discovered significant lithium reserves in Jammu and Kashmir, which could be a game-changer for its EV ambitions.

articles.headline: Renowned Film Director A. T. Raghu Passes Away at 75
articles.explainer:
• Veteran film director A. T. Raghu, known for his significant contributions to regional cinema, passed away at the age of 75.
• Raghu directed numerous critically acclaimed and commercially successful films, primarily in the South Indian film industry.
• He was celebrated for his unique storytelling and ability to nurture new talent.
• His demise marks a loss for the Indian film fraternity, leaving behind a rich legacy of cinematic work.
• He often explored themes of social relevance and cultural identity in his movies.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Modern Indian history from about the middle of the eighteenth century until the present- significant events, personalities, issues.
prelimsPoints: A. T. Raghu was a prominent figure in South Indian cinema.
Indian cinema is one of the largest film industries in the world.
The Dadasaheb Phalke Award is India's highest honor in cinema.
articles.imageDescription: Black and white portrait of A. T. Raghu, commemorating his legacy in Indian cinema.
articles.trivia: The first full-length Indian feature film was 'Raja Harishchandra', directed by Dadasaheb Phalke, released in 1913.

articles.headline: Veteran Politician Devendra Sharma Passes Away at 66
articles.explainer:
• Devendra Sharma, a seasoned politician and former Member of Parliament/Legislative Assembly, passed away at 66.
• Sharma had a long and distinguished career in public service, advocating for various social and economic causes.
• He was known for his grassroots connect and his efforts towards rural development.
• His passing is mourned by his constituents and political colleagues across party lines.
• He held several important portfolios during his political tenure.
gsSubTopics: Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
Salient features of the Representation of People's Act.
prelimsPoints: The minimum age to be a Member of Parliament (Lok Sabha) is 25 years.
The minimum age to be a Member of Legislative Assembly is 25 years.
Parliamentary elections are conducted by the Election Commission of India.
articles.imageDescription: Formal portrait of Devendra Sharma, representing his political career.
articles.trivia: The first general elections in India saw 14 national parties and 39 state parties contest.

articles.headline: Veteran Actor Rakesh Pandey Passes Away at 77
articles.explainer:
• Veteran actor Rakesh Pandey, known for his versatile roles in Hindi cinema and television, passed away at the age of 77.
• Pandey had a career spanning over five decades, starring in numerous films and popular TV serials.
• He was admired for his nuanced performances and contribution to the entertainment industry.
• His demise has been mourned by colleagues and fans across the country.
• He often portrayed character roles that left a lasting impact.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
prelimsPoints: Bollywood is the Hindi-language film industry based in Mumbai.
Indian television began broadcasting in 1959.
The Filmfare Awards are one of the oldest and most prominent film awards in India.
articles.imageDescription: Still image of Rakesh Pandey from one of his memorable film roles, celebrating his acting career.
articles.trivia: The first television broadcast in India was a modest experimental transmission from Delhi in 1959.

articles.headline: India Launches National Environmental Performance Index to Track Sustainability Goals
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change launched India's own National Environmental Performance Index (NEPI).
• NEPI aims to comprehensively assess states' and union territories' progress on various environmental parameters.
• The index will cover air quality, water management, biodiversity conservation, waste management, and climate action.
• It is designed to foster competitive federalism in environmental governance and provide data-driven policy insights.
• The initiative seeks to align national environmental efforts with global sustainability goals and commitments.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
articles.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change is the nodal agency.
The index evaluates states and UTs on predefined environmental indicators.
It is distinct from the global Environmental Performance Index (EPI) released by Yale and Columbia Universities.
Parameters include forest cover, renewable energy adoption, pollution levels, and waste processing.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of launching a National Environmental Performance Index (NEPI) for India. How can such an index drive better environmental governance and policy implementation at the state level?
Analyze the key environmental challenges India faces today and how NEPI can provide a framework for targeted interventions and monitoring progress towards sustainability goals.
Examine the potential for 'competitive federalism' in environmental protection fostered by such an index. What are the benefits and potential drawbacks of this approach?
Evaluate how India's environmental policies and indices align with its international commitments under agreements like the Paris Agreement and Sustainable Development Goals.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic showing a stylized map of India with environmental indicators (tree, water drop, sun) around it, representing the Environmental Performance Index.
articles.trivia: India is a signatory to the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) and the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC).

articles.headline: India Witnesses Significant Surge in Global Patent Filings, Reflecting Innovation Push
articles.explainer:
• A new report by the World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO) indicates a substantial increase in global patent filings from India.
• The surge reflects India's growing innovation ecosystem, driven by government initiatives and private sector R&D investments.
• Key sectors contributing to this rise include information technology, pharmaceuticals, biotechnology, and renewable energy.
• The report highlights India's potential to become a global hub for innovation and technological development.
• Challenges remain in translating patents into commercial success and improving research infrastructure.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: WIPO is one of the 15 specialized agencies of the United Nations.
India is a member of WIPO and a signatory to the Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT).
A patent grants exclusive rights to an inventor for an invention.
The Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) is the nodal department for IPR in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the factors contributing to India's recent surge in global patent filings. How does this reflect the country's evolving innovation landscape and its aspirations to become a knowledge economy?
Analyze the importance of a robust Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) regime for fostering innovation and attracting investment in research and development.
Examine the challenges India faces in translating increased patent filings into tangible economic growth and commercial success. What policy interventions are needed to bridge this gap?
Evaluate the role of government initiatives like 'Make in India' and 'Startup India' in promoting innovation and enhancing India's global competitiveness in technology-intensive sectors.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized graph showing an upward trend of patent symbols, with a magnifying glass over India's map, representing innovation and intellectual property.
articles.trivia: The first patent in India was granted in 1856 under Act VI of 1856, related to the 'exclusive privilege' of inventions.

articles.headline: Archaeologists Uncover New Insights into Harappan Urban Planning at Recently Discovered Site
articles.explainer:
• A team of archaeologists announced significant discoveries at a newly excavated Harappan site in Gujarat.
• The findings shed new light on the advanced urban planning, water management systems, and craftsmanship of the Indus Valley Civilization.
• Evidence suggests a well-structured settlement with intricate drainage systems and sophisticated pottery.
• The site provides crucial data for understanding the socio-economic organization and trade networks of the Harappan people.
• Further excavations are expected to reveal more about the unique characteristics of this particular Harappan city.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
The Harappan/Indus Valley Civilization.

articles.headline: Ministry of Tourism Unveils 'Sustainable Tourism Promotion Policy' for Eco-friendly Growth
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tourism launched a new policy aimed at promoting sustainable and responsible tourism across India.
• The policy focuses on eco-tourism, cultural heritage preservation, and minimizing the environmental footprint of tourism activities.
• It encourages community-based tourism models, ensuring local populations benefit from tourist influx.
• Emphasis is placed on digital promotion, skill development in green tourism, and robust infrastructure for eco-friendly travel.
• The policy seeks to position India as a leading destination for sustainable tourism globally.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: The Ministry of Tourism is responsible for the formulation and administration of rules and regulations and laws relating to tourism in India.
Eco-tourism focuses on responsible travel to natural areas that conserves the environment and improves the well-being of local people.
The 'Incredible India' campaign is a major promotional initiative by the Ministry of Tourism.
Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth) and 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production) are relevant.
mainsPoints: Discuss the concept of sustainable tourism and its importance for India, considering its diverse natural and cultural heritage. How does the new policy aim to achieve this balance?
Analyze the economic, environmental, and social benefits of promoting eco-tourism and community-based tourism models in India. What challenges might arise in their implementation?
Examine the role of infrastructure development, digital promotion, and skill enhancement in strengthening India's position as a global leader in sustainable tourism.
Evaluate the potential of tourism to contribute to local livelihoods and regional development, while also preserving cultural identity and ecological integrity.
articles.imageDescription: A collage of diverse Indian landscapes (mountains, beaches, heritage sites) with green leaves overlay, symbolizing sustainable tourism.
articles.trivia: India's tourism sector is one of the largest service industries in the country.

articles.headline: India Proposes National Framework for Ethical AI Development and Deployment
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) released a draft National Framework for Ethical Artificial Intelligence (AI).
• The framework proposes guidelines for responsible AI development, focusing on fairness, accountability, transparency, and safety.
• It aims to establish India as a leader in ethical AI, ensuring AI technologies benefit society without causing harm.
• Key areas addressed include data privacy, algorithmic bias, human oversight, and regulatory mechanisms for AI applications.
• Stakeholder consultations are invited to finalize the framework, which will guide future AI policies and innovations.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.

Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
prelimsPoints: MeitY (Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology) is the nodal ministry for AI policy.
Ethical AI focuses on developing AI systems that are fair, accountable, and transparent.
Algorithmic bias refers to systematic and repeatable errors in a computer system that create unfair outcomes.
India is a member of the Global Partnership on Artificial Intelligence (GPAI).
mainsPoints: Discuss the imperative for an ethical AI framework in India, considering the rapid advancements and pervasive applications of Artificial Intelligence. What are the key ethical challenges posed by AI?

Analyze the core principles of the proposed National Framework for Ethical AI development and deployment. How do these principles address concerns related to fairness, accountability, and transparency?
Examine the potential socio-economic implications of unethical AI, including issues of job displacement, privacy infringement, and societal discrimination. How can policy mitigate these risks?
Evaluate India's potential to lead in ethical AI development on the global stage. What steps are required to foster a responsible AI ecosystem that balances innovation with societal well-being?
articles.imageDescription: A stylized brain with interconnected nodes and lines, representing Artificial Intelligence and its ethical considerations.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956 at the Dartmouth Conference.

highlights: Supreme Court mandates greater transparency in electoral funding, setting new ECI guidelines.
Major anti-Naxal operation in Chhattisgarh neutralizes 30 Naxalites; one security personnel martyred.
India-Japan 2+2 Dialogue strengthens Indo-Pacific cooperation, focusing on defence and emerging tech.
PM launches Phase II of PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana, targeting 2 crore households for rooftop solar.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the recent Supreme Court ruling on electoral funding, consider the following statements:
1. The ruling mandates the Election Commission of India to formulate stringent guidelines for disclosure of political donations.
2. The judgment emphasizes the 'right to know' of citizens regarding sources of political funding as part of Article 21.
3. Electoral Bonds were declared unconstitutional in an earlier Supreme Court judgment in 2024.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct as the ruling directs ECI for new guidelines. Statement 3 is correct as the Supreme Court indeed declared Electoral Bonds unconstitutional in February 2024. Statement 2 is incorrect because while the 'right to know' is emphasized, it is generally derived from Article 19(1)(a) (freedom of speech and expression), not primarily Article 21 (right to life and personal liberty) in this specific context, although both are fundamental rights.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements regarding the Indus Water Treaty (IWT) is/are correct?
1. The IWT was brokered by the United Nations in 1960.
2. Under the treaty, India has exclusive rights to the waters of the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej).
3. The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is a bilateral body responsible for implementing the treaty.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect; the IWT was brokered by the World Bank, not the United Nations. Statement 2 is correct, India has exclusive rights over the Eastern Rivers. Statement 3 is correct, the PIC is the bilateral body for treaty implementation.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements about PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana:
1. It is a central sector scheme launched by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy.
2. The scheme aims to provide up to 300 units of free electricity per month to eligible households through rooftop solar installations.
3. It offers significant subsidies and collateral-free bank loans for beneficiaries.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. The PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana is indeed a central sector scheme under MNRE, aims to provide free electricity up to 300 units, and offers subsidies and collateral-free loans to promote rooftop solar adoption.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following is NOT a core principle typically associated with Ethical AI frameworks?
(a) Fairness and Non-Discrimination
(b) Transparency and Explainability
(c) Human Control and Oversight
(d) Maximizing Profit at All Costs
prelimsMocks.explanation: Ethical AI frameworks universally prioritize principles like fairness, transparency, accountability, and human oversight to ensure AI systems are developed and used responsibly. Maximizing profit at all costs is a business objective that often conflicts with ethical considerations, making it an antithetical principle to ethical AI.

prelimsMocks.question:
Match the following Harappan sites with their respective states:
1. Dholavira		A. Haryana
2. Rakhigarhi		B. Gujarat
3. Kalibangan		C. Rajasthan
Select the correct matching code:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Dholavira is in Gujarat. Rakhigarhi is in Haryana. Kalibangan is in Rajasthan. Therefore, the correct match is 1-B, 2-A, 3-C.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's latest pronouncement on electoral funding transparency marks a significant step towards strengthening democratic accountability. Critically analyze the implications of this ruling on India's political finance landscape and suggest further reforms needed to ensure truly free and fair elections. (150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution—evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure; Salient features of the Representation of People's Act.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the SC ruling and its focus on transparency. Discuss implications like increased accountability for parties, potential impact on corporate funding, and citizen's right to know. Suggest further reforms such as state funding of elections, stricter penalties for violations, and real-time disclosure mechanisms to enhance the electoral process.

mainsMocks.question: Despite significant efforts, Left-Wing Extremism (LWE) continues to pose a formidable internal security challenge in parts of India. Discuss the multi-pronged strategy adopted by the government to combat LWE, highlighting both its successes and persistent limitations. (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Linkages between development and spread of extremism; Challenges to internal security through communication networks; Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
mainsMocks.approach: Define LWE and its geographical spread. Elaborate on the multi-pronged strategy: security operations (kinetic approach), development initiatives (addressing root causes), governance reforms, and surrender & rehabilitation policies. Discuss successes (reduction in violence, area of influence) and limitations (ideological resilience, terrain challenges, intelligence gaps, uneven development). Conclude with need for sustained, holistic approach.

mainsMocks.question: India's push for sustainable tourism, as evidenced by the new 'Sustainable Tourism Promotion Policy', reflects a global shift towards responsible travel. Examine the potential benefits of this policy for India's economy and environment, and identify the key challenges in its effective implementation. (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment; Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the new policy and the concept of sustainable tourism. Discuss economic benefits: job creation, local income generation, diversified tourism products, enhanced international image. Discuss environmental benefits: conservation of natural resources, reduced pollution, biodiversity protection. Identify challenges: lack of awareness, infrastructure deficits, managing tourist influx, balancing development with conservation, ensuring community participation, and funding.

GS1.headline: Renowned Film Director A. T. Raghu Passes Away at 75
GS1.explainer:
• Veteran film director A. T. Raghu, known for his significant contributions to regional cinema, passed away at the age of 75.
• Raghu directed numerous critically acclaimed and commercially successful films, primarily in the South Indian film industry.
• He was celebrated for his unique storytelling and ability to nurture new talent.
• His demise marks a loss for the Indian film fraternity, leaving behind a rich legacy of cinematic work.
• He often explored themes of social relevance and cultural identity in his movies.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Modern Indian history from about the middle of the eighteenth century until the present- significant events, personalities, issues.
prelimsPoints: A. T. Raghu was a prominent figure in South Indian cinema.
Indian cinema is one of the largest film industries in the world.
The Dadasaheb Phalke Award is India's highest honor in cinema.

GS1.trivia: The first full-length Indian feature film was 'Raja Harishchandra', directed by Dadasaheb Phalke, released in 1913.

GS1.explainer:

• He was admired for his nuanced performances and contribution to the entertainment industry.
• His demise has been mourned by colleagues and fans across the country.
• He often portrayed character roles that left a lasting impact.

Indian television began broadcasting in 1959.
The Filmfare Awards are one of the oldest and most prominent film awards in India.

GS1.headline: Archaeologists Uncover New Insights into Harappan Urban Planning at Recently Discovered Site
GS1.explainer:
• A team of archaeologists announced significant discoveries at a newly excavated Harappan site in Gujarat.
• The findings shed new light on the advanced urban planning, water management systems, and craftsmanship of the Indus Valley Civilization.
• Evidence suggests a well-structured settlement with intricate drainage systems and sophisticated pottery.
• The site provides crucial data for understanding the socio-economic organization and trade networks of the Harappan people.
• Further excavations are expected to reveal more about the unique characteristics of this particular Harappan city.

The Harappan/Indus Valley Civilization.

Major Harappan sites include Harappa, Mohenjo-Daro, Lothal, Dholavira, Rakhigarhi, and Kalibangan.
Gujarat hosts several important Harappan sites like Lothal and Dholavira.
The Harappans were known for their sophisticated urban planning, grid patterns, and drainage systems.

Analyze how recent archaeological discoveries, such as the one in Gujarat, contribute to our understanding of the Harappan civilization's socio-economic structure, trade, and cultural practices.
Examine the causes for the decline of the Indus Valley Civilization based on archaeological evidence and prevailing theories.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Reaffirms Transparency in Electoral Funding, Sets New Guidelines
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark ruling, emphasized the paramount importance of transparency in electoral funding to uphold democratic principles.
• The judgment directed the Election Commission of India (ECI) to formulate new, stringent guidelines for disclosure of political donations.
• This ruling comes as a follow-up to previous judgments and aims to close loopholes in existing electoral finance laws.
• The court highlighted the 'right to know' of citizens regarding sources of political funding as an integral part of free and fair elections.
• Implications include potential reforms in the Representation of the People Act and increased accountability for political parties.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
Salient features of the Representation of People's Act.
Role of Election Commission of India.
prelimsPoints: The Supreme Court's ruling impacts the Election Commission of India's powers and responsibilities.
The judgment refers to previous rulings on electoral transparency, potentially including the 2018 and 2024 judgments on electoral bonds.
Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution (Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression) is often cited in 'right to know' cases.
The Representation of the People Act, 1951 governs elections in India.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the impact of the Supreme Court's verdict on the financial transparency of political parties and its role in strengthening Indian democracy.
Discuss the challenges in implementing comprehensive electoral finance reforms in India, considering the interplay of political will, legal frameworks, and practical enforcement.
Examine how the 'right to know' regarding political funding aligns with and potentially strengthens other fundamental rights guaranteed by the Indian Constitution.
Evaluate the role of the Election Commission of India in ensuring free and fair elections, particularly in light of judicial pronouncements on electoral transparency.
GS2.imageDescription: Illustration of the Supreme Court of India building with scales of justice, symbolizing judicial pronouncements on electoral transparency.
GS2.trivia: The first general elections in independent India were held from October 1951 to February 1952, under the provisions of the Representation of the People Act, 1951.

GS2.headline: India-Japan 2+2 Dialogue Concludes, Strengthening Indo-Pacific Cooperation
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-Japan 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue involving Foreign and Defence Ministers concluded in Tokyo.
• Discussions focused on enhancing bilateral security and defence cooperation, including joint exercises and technology sharing.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region, countering unilateral attempts to change the status quo.
• Key agreements were reached on expanding cooperation in emerging technologies, cyber security, and critical infrastructure.
• The dialogue also addressed regional and global issues, including climate change and counter-terrorism efforts.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: The 2+2 Dialogue involves the Foreign and Defence Ministers of both countries.
India has 2+2 dialogues with the USA, Japan, Australia, Russia, and the UK.
Japan is a key partner in the Quad grouping, alongside India, the USA, and Australia.
The 'Act East Policy' is a cornerstone of India's engagement with Southeast Asian and East Asian countries, including Japan.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the India-Japan strategic partnership in ensuring stability and prosperity in the Indo-Pacific region. How does this partnership contribute to India's 'Act East Policy'?
Discuss the evolving dimensions of defence and security cooperation between India and Japan. What are the potential areas for further collaboration, especially in emerging technologies?
Evaluate the role of 2+2 dialogues as a diplomatic mechanism for enhancing bilateral relations and addressing complex regional and global challenges.
Examine how India's bilateral relations with key partners like Japan align with its broader geopolitical objectives and its stance on multilateral forums.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan side-by-side, symbolizing strong bilateral ties and cooperation in the Indo-Pacific.
GS2.trivia: Japan is a significant investor in India's infrastructure projects, including the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail (Bullet Train) project.

GS2.headline: Gender Budgeting Report 2024-25 Highlights Progress and Gaps in Women-Centric Schemes
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Finance released its annual Gender Budgeting Statement as part of the Union Budget 2024-25.
• The report details allocations for schemes specifically targeting women's empowerment and gender equality across various ministries.
• While there's an increase in overall allocation, the report also identified critical gaps in implementation and outreach.
• Key focus areas included women's health, education, economic participation, and safety.
• The analysis emphasized the need for better data collection and outcome-based monitoring for gender-responsive budgeting.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
prelimsPoints: Gender Budgeting is a tool to ensure that government policies and programs address gender inequalities.
It is not a separate budget but an analysis of the government's main budget from a gender perspective.
The first Gender Budget Statement in India was introduced in 2005-06.
Part A of the Gender Budget Statement covers schemes 100% for women; Part B covers schemes where at least 30% of allocation is for women.
mainsPoints: Discuss the concept of Gender Budgeting and its evolution in India. How effective has it been in promoting gender equality and women's empowerment?
Analyze the key findings of the latest Gender Budgeting Report, highlighting both the successes and the persistent challenges in achieving gender-responsive development outcomes.
Examine the role of various stakeholders, including government ministries, civil society organizations, and local communities, in ensuring effective implementation of women-centric schemes.
Suggest reforms and policy measures to strengthen gender budgeting mechanisms and improve the impact of welfare schemes for women in India.
GS2.imageDescription: Infographic showing various sectors like health, education, and economy, with a focus on women, representing gender budgeting.
GS2.trivia: The idea of gender budgeting originated in Australia in the mid-1980s.

GS2.headline: Review of National Education Policy 2020 Implementation Highlights Progress and Implementation Hurdles
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education released an interim review report on the implementation progress of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• The report noted significant strides in curriculum reforms, digital education initiatives, and skill development programs.
• However, it also pointed out challenges in teacher training, infrastructure development, and equitable access in remote areas.
• The review emphasized the need for greater state-level coordination and resource allocation to achieve NEP's ambitious goals.
• Recommendations included accelerating the establishment of multidisciplinary institutions and strengthening early childhood care and education.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: The National Education Policy 2020 replaced the 1986 policy.
NEP 2020 aims for universalization of education from pre-school to secondary level.
It proposes a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure instead of the 10+2 system.
The policy emphasizes vocational education and multidisciplinary learning.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the progress and challenges in the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020. What specific reforms are crucial for its successful realization?
Discuss how NEP 2020 aims to transform India's education system to meet 21st-century demands, focusing on aspects like skill development, digital literacy, and holistic learning.
Examine the role of technology and digital initiatives in enhancing educational access and quality as envisioned by the NEP. What are the associated equity concerns?
Analyze the importance of teacher training and capacity building for the effective implementation of the new pedagogical approaches proposed by NEP 2020.
GS2.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing the transformative goals of the National Education Policy.
GS2.trivia: The Kothari Commission (1964-66) was instrumental in shaping India's first National Policy on Education in 1968.

GS2.headline: India-Pakistan Hold Talks on Indus Water Treaty, Addressing Technical Issues
GS2.explainer:
• Delegations from India and Pakistan met for their annual Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) talks under the Indus Water Treaty (IWT).
• The discussions primarily focused on technical issues related to various hydroelectric projects on the western rivers.
• India reiterated its commitment to the treaty while asserting its right to utilize its share of water resources for non-consumptive uses.
• Pakistan raised concerns over the design of certain Indian projects, which India clarified were within treaty provisions.
• The talks are crucial for maintaining peace and cooperation over shared water resources, despite broader bilateral tensions.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: The Indus Water Treaty was signed in 1960, brokered by the World Bank.
It allocates the waters of the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and the Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan.
India has the right to use the waters of the Western Rivers for non-consumptive purposes like power generation.
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is a bilateral commission that implements and manages the treaty.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical significance and continued relevance of the Indus Water Treaty (IWT) in managing water resources between India and Pakistan. What makes it one of the most successful water treaties globally?

Analyze the challenges and complexities in implementing the IWT, particularly concerning the construction of hydroelectric projects on the Western Rivers. How does India balance its development needs with treaty obligations?
Examine the role of water diplomacy in mitigating tensions between riparian states. How can the IWT serve as a model for resolving other transboundary water disputes?
Evaluate the potential impact of climate change on the Indus Basin and the implications for the long-term sustainability of the IWT.
GS2.imageDescription: Map of the Indus River system showing the allocation of rivers between India and Pakistan, symbolizing the Indus Water Treaty.
GS2.trivia: The Indus Water Treaty is one of the very few international treaties that have survived multiple wars and conflicts between the signatory nations.

GS2.headline: National Digital Health Mission Expands Reach, Focuses on Telemedicine Integration
GS2.explainer:
• The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM) announced plans to expand its services to cover more remote and rural areas.
• A key focus of the expansion is the seamless integration of telemedicine services into the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) framework.
• This aims to improve access to healthcare, reduce geographical barriers, and enhance the efficiency of medical consultations.
• The mission emphasized creating a robust digital health ecosystem, including health IDs, health professional registries, and health facility registries.
• Training programs for healthcare workers on digital platforms are being scaled up to support the increased adoption of digital health solutions.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: The National Digital Health Mission (NDHM) was launched in August 2020, later renamed Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM).
It aims to create a digital health ecosystem in India.
A key component is the Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) number, a unique health ID.
e-Sanjeevani is a national telemedicine service managed by the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of the National Digital Health Mission (ABDM) in revolutionizing healthcare delivery in India, particularly in terms of accessibility, efficiency, and equity.
Analyze the role of telemedicine in bridging the rural-urban divide in healthcare and its specific applications during public health crises.
Examine the challenges in implementing a comprehensive digital health ecosystem in India, including data privacy concerns, digital literacy, and infrastructure gaps.
Suggest measures to ensure the inclusivity and robustness of the National Digital Health Mission, making it beneficial for all sections of society.
GS2.imageDescription: A graphic depicting a doctor consulting a patient via a digital screen, symbolizing telemedicine and digital health.
GS2.trivia: India has one of the largest populations of internet users, which provides a strong foundation for digital health initiatives.

GS2.headline: Veteran Politician Devendra Sharma Passes Away at 66
GS2.explainer:
• Devendra Sharma, a seasoned politician and former Member of Parliament/Legislative Assembly, passed away at 66.
• Sharma had a long and distinguished career in public service, advocating for various social and economic causes.
• He was known for his grassroots connect and his efforts towards rural development.
• His passing is mourned by his constituents and political colleagues across party lines.
• He held several important portfolios during his political tenure.
gsSubTopics: Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
Salient features of the Representation of People's Act.
prelimsPoints: The minimum age to be a Member of Parliament (Lok Sabha) is 25 years.
The minimum age to be a Member of Legislative Assembly is 25 years.
Parliamentary elections are conducted by the Election Commission of India.
GS2.imageDescription: Formal portrait of Devendra Sharma, representing his political career.
GS2.trivia: The first general elections in India saw 14 national parties and 39 state parties contest.

GS3.headline: Major Anti-Naxal Operation in Chhattisgarh Claims Lives of 30 Naxalites and One Security Personnel
GS3.explainer:
• Intense clashes erupted between security forces and Naxalites in Dantewada and Kanker districts of Chhattisgarh.
• The operation resulted in the neutralization of 30 Naxalites, a significant blow to the insurgent group's presence in the region.
• Tragically, one military personnel also lost their life during the fierce encounter.
• The operation involved joint forces of state police and central paramilitary units, acting on specific intelligence inputs.
• Authorities have intensified combing operations in the affected areas, underscoring ongoing efforts to curb left-wing extremism.
gsSubTopics: Linkages between development and spread of extremism.
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security.

Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
prelimsPoints: Dantewada and Kanker districts are located in the Bastar region of Chhattisgarh, known for Naxalite activities.
Left-Wing Extremism (LWE) is primarily concentrated in the 'Red Corridor' regions of India.
Security forces involved in anti-Naxal operations include CRPF, BSF, ITBP, and state police forces.
Operation Green Hunt was a major anti-Naxal offensive launched by the government in 2009-10.
mainsPoints: Analyze the socio-economic factors contributing to the persistence of Left-Wing Extremism (LWE) in regions like Chhattisgarh, and suggest comprehensive strategies for addressing them.
Discuss the effectiveness of current security strategies in combating Naxalism. What role do development initiatives play alongside kinetic operations?
Examine the challenges faced by security forces in anti-Naxal operations, including terrain, local support, and logistical issues.
Critically evaluate the 'Surrender and Rehabilitation Policy' for Naxalites. How effective has it been in de-radicalizing and reintegrating former cadres?
GS3.imageDescription: Map highlighting Dantewada and Kanker districts in Chhattisgarh, symbolizing the ongoing security challenges in the region.
GS3.trivia: The term 'Naxalite' originated from the village of Naxalbari in West Bengal, where a peasant uprising took place in 1967.

GS3.headline: Prime Minister Launches Phase II of PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana, Targets 2 Crore Households
GS3.explainer:
• The Prime Minister today launched the second phase of the PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana, a flagship rooftop solar scheme.
• This phase aims to cover an additional 2 crore households, providing free electricity and promoting renewable energy adoption.
• The scheme offers significant subsidies and collateral-free bank loans for installing rooftop solar systems.
• It is expected to boost employment generation in the renewable energy sector and reduce India's carbon footprint.
• Emphasis was placed on achieving energy self-sufficiency and reducing electricity bills for economically weaker sections.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
