The scheme provides financial assistance for installing rooftop solar panels.
It aims to provide up to 300 units of free electricity per month to eligible households.
The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) is the nodal ministry for this scheme.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the potential of rooftop solar programs like PM-Surya Ghar to address India's energy demands and contribute to its climate change commitments. What are the associated challenges?
Discuss the socio-economic benefits of promoting decentralized renewable energy generation, especially for rural and economically vulnerable populations.
Examine the role of government subsidies and financial incentives in accelerating the adoption of renewable energy technologies in India.
Analyze the impact of such schemes on India's energy security and its transition away from fossil fuels.
GS3.imageDescription: An illustration of solar panels on rooftops of houses, symbolizing the PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana.
GS3.trivia: India's installed renewable energy capacity (excluding large hydro) is the 4th largest in the world.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches New Ocean Monitoring Satellite for Climate Research
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched a new state-of-the-art ocean monitoring satellite aboard the PSLV-C59.
• The satellite, named 'OceanSat-4', is designed to provide critical data for oceanography, climate studies, and marine weather forecasting.
• It carries advanced sensors for measuring sea surface temperature, ocean color, and wind vectors, vital for understanding ocean dynamics.
• This mission reinforces India's capabilities in remote sensing and its commitment to global climate action.
• The data from OceanSat-4 will be crucial for disaster management, fisheries, and coastal zone management.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) is ISRO's third-generation launch vehicle.
OceanSat-4 is part of India's series of ocean observation satellites.
The satellite will contribute to international efforts for ocean monitoring and climate modeling.
ISRO operates from the Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC) in Sriharikota, Andhra Pradesh.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's space program, particularly its Earth Observation Satellites, in addressing critical national challenges such as climate change, disaster management, and resource mapping.
Evaluate ISRO's role in advancing scientific research and technological innovation. How does its indigenization efforts contribute to India's self-reliance in space technology?
Examine the applications of satellite data in oceanography and its importance for sustainable development, including fisheries management and coastal protection.
Analyze the global cooperation in space exploration and climate research, highlighting India's contributions and collaborations.
GS3.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of the OceanSat-4 satellite orbiting Earth, with oceans visible below.
GS3.trivia: ISRO was formed in 1969, succeeding the Indian National Committee for Space Research (INCOSPAR), which was established in 1962.

GS3.headline: Draft National Water Policy 2025 Emphasizes Integrated River Basin Management
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Jal Shakti released the draft National Water Policy 2025 for public consultation.
• The policy prioritizes integrated river basin management, inter-state water cooperation, and demand-side management.
• It proposes a shift from a supply-side approach to water management to one focused on conservation, reuse, and efficiency.
• Climate change adaptation, groundwater recharge, and protection of water bodies are key pillars of the new policy.
• The draft also advocates for greater community participation and use of modern technologies for water governance.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: The Ministry of Jal Shakti was formed in 2019 by merging the Ministry of Water Resources, River Development & Ganga Rejuvenation and Ministry of Drinking Water and Sanitation.
India's first National Water Policy was adopted in 1987, revised in 2002 and 2012.
Integrated River Basin Management aims to coordinate management of water, land, and related resources across a river basin.
The concept of 'water neutrality' or 'water positive' is gaining traction.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the proposed National Water Policy 2025. How far does it address India's persistent water challenges, including scarcity, pollution, and inter-state disputes?
Discuss the importance of shifting from a supply-side to a demand-side approach in water management, emphasizing the role of community participation and technological innovation.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in implementing integrated river basin management, particularly in the context of India's federal structure and diverse hydrological regions.
Evaluate the potential impact of climate change on India's water resources and how the new policy aims to build resilience against these impacts.
GS3.imageDescription: A stylized map of India with major rivers highlighted, symbolizing integrated water resource management.
GS3.trivia: India accounts for about 18% of the world's population but has only 4% of the world's renewable water resources.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Inflation Concerns, Focus on Growth
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) decided to keep the repo rate unchanged at its current level.
• The decision was influenced by persistent inflation concerns, particularly in food and core inflation, despite some moderation.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to withdrawing accommodation to ensure inflation aligns with the target while supporting growth.
• Analysts anticipate continued vigilance from the RBI, with future rate actions dependent on inflation trajectory and economic data.
• The Governor highlighted global economic uncertainties and geopolitical tensions as key risks to domestic growth and inflation outlook.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body, with three members from the RBI and three external members appointed by the government.
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
The target for inflation is 4% with a band of +/- 2% (2% to 6%).
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks in the event of any shortfall of funds.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind the RBI's decision to maintain the status quo on the repo rate. How does this decision balance the objectives of inflation control and economic growth?
Discuss the challenges faced by the Monetary Policy Committee in managing inflation amidst global supply chain disruptions and domestic demand pressures.
Examine the various tools of monetary policy employed by the RBI and their effectiveness in achieving macro-economic stability.
Evaluate the impact of the RBI's monetary policy stance on various sectors of the Indian economy, including banking, industry, and consumption.
GS3.imageDescription: The Reserve Bank of India building with a graph showing stable interest rates, representing monetary policy decisions.
GS3.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

GS3.headline: Government Unveils New Policy for Exploration and Mining of Critical Minerals
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Mines announced a new policy framework aimed at accelerating the exploration and mining of critical minerals in India.
• The policy introduces incentives for private sector participation and foreign investment in deep-seated and strategic mineral blocks.
• It emphasizes sustainable mining practices, environmental protection, and local community engagement.
• The move is crucial for reducing India's import dependence on critical minerals like lithium, cobalt, and rare earth elements.
• The policy seeks to bolster domestic manufacturing capabilities in sectors like electric vehicles, renewable energy, and advanced electronics.

Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Critical minerals are essential for economic development and national security, especially in high-tech industries.
India heavily relies on imports for many critical minerals.
The Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Act, 1957, is the principal legislation governing mining in India.
The Geological Survey of India (GSI) is the primary body for geological mapping and mineral exploration.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of critical minerals for India's economic growth and national security. How does the new mining policy aim to achieve self-reliance in this sector?
Analyze the challenges involved in the exploration and extraction of critical minerals in India, including environmental concerns, technological gaps, and regulatory hurdles.
Evaluate the role of private sector participation and foreign investment in boosting India's critical mineral supply chain. What incentives are crucial for attracting such investments?
Examine the linkages between critical mineral availability and the growth of key industries like electric vehicles, renewable energy, and advanced electronics in India.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustrative image of various critical minerals (lithium, cobalt, rare earths) arranged around a map of India, symbolizing their strategic importance.
GS3.trivia: India recently discovered significant lithium reserves in Jammu and Kashmir, which could be a game-changer for its EV ambitions.

GS3.headline: India Launches National Environmental Performance Index to Track Sustainability Goals
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change launched India's own National Environmental Performance Index (NEPI).
• NEPI aims to comprehensively assess states' and union territories' progress on various environmental parameters.
• The index will cover air quality, water management, biodiversity conservation, waste management, and climate action.
• It is designed to foster competitive federalism in environmental governance and provide data-driven policy insights.
• The initiative seeks to align national environmental efforts with global sustainability goals and commitments.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
GS3.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change is the nodal agency.
The index evaluates states and UTs on predefined environmental indicators.
It is distinct from the global Environmental Performance Index (EPI) released by Yale and Columbia Universities.
Parameters include forest cover, renewable energy adoption, pollution levels, and waste processing.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of launching a National Environmental Performance Index (NEPI) for India. How can such an index drive better environmental governance and policy implementation at the state level?
Analyze the key environmental challenges India faces today and how NEPI can provide a framework for targeted interventions and monitoring progress towards sustainability goals.
Examine the potential for 'competitive federalism' in environmental protection fostered by such an index. What are the benefits and potential drawbacks of this approach?
Evaluate how India's environmental policies and indices align with its international commitments under agreements like the Paris Agreement and Sustainable Development Goals.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic showing a stylized map of India with environmental indicators (tree, water drop, sun) around it, representing the Environmental Performance Index.
GS3.trivia: India is a signatory to the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) and the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC).

GS3.headline: India Witnesses Significant Surge in Global Patent Filings, Reflecting Innovation Push
GS3.explainer:
• A new report by the World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO) indicates a substantial increase in global patent filings from India.
• The surge reflects India's growing innovation ecosystem, driven by government initiatives and private sector R&D investments.
• Key sectors contributing to this rise include information technology, pharmaceuticals, biotechnology, and renewable energy.
• The report highlights India's potential to become a global hub for innovation and technological development.
• Challenges remain in translating patents into commercial success and improving research infrastructure.

Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: WIPO is one of the 15 specialized agencies of the United Nations.
India is a member of WIPO and a signatory to the Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT).
A patent grants exclusive rights to an inventor for an invention.
The Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) is the nodal department for IPR in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the factors contributing to India's recent surge in global patent filings. How does this reflect the country's evolving innovation landscape and its aspirations to become a knowledge economy?
Analyze the importance of a robust Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) regime for fostering innovation and attracting investment in research and development.
Examine the challenges India faces in translating increased patent filings into tangible economic growth and commercial success. What policy interventions are needed to bridge this gap?
Evaluate the role of government initiatives like 'Make in India' and 'Startup India' in promoting innovation and enhancing India's global competitiveness in technology-intensive sectors.
GS3.imageDescription: A stylized graph showing an upward trend of patent symbols, with a magnifying glass over India's map, representing innovation and intellectual property.
GS3.trivia: The first patent in India was granted in 1856 under Act VI of 1856, related to the 'exclusive privilege' of inventions.

GS3.headline: Ministry of Tourism Unveils 'Sustainable Tourism Promotion Policy' for Eco-friendly Growth
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tourism launched a new policy aimed at promoting sustainable and responsible tourism across India.
• The policy focuses on eco-tourism, cultural heritage preservation, and minimizing the environmental footprint of tourism activities.
• It encourages community-based tourism models, ensuring local populations benefit from tourist influx.
• Emphasis is placed on digital promotion, skill development in green tourism, and robust infrastructure for eco-friendly travel.
• The policy seeks to position India as a leading destination for sustainable tourism globally.

Eco-tourism focuses on responsible travel to natural areas that conserves the environment and improves the well-being of local people.

Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth) and 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production) are relevant.

GS3.trivia: India's tourism sector is one of the largest service industries in the country.

GS3.headline: India Proposes National Framework for Ethical AI Development and Deployment
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) released a draft National Framework for Ethical Artificial Intelligence (AI).
• The framework proposes guidelines for responsible AI development, focusing on fairness, accountability, transparency, and safety.
• It aims to establish India as a leader in ethical AI, ensuring AI technologies benefit society without causing harm.
• Key areas addressed include data privacy, algorithmic bias, human oversight, and regulatory mechanisms for AI applications.
• Stakeholder consultations are invited to finalize the framework, which will guide future AI policies and innovations.

Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.

India is a member of the Global Partnership on Artificial Intelligence (GPAI).

Examine the potential socio-economic implications of unethical AI, including issues of job displacement, privacy infringement, and societal discrimination. How can policy mitigate these risks?

GS3.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956 at the Dartmouth Conference.
